title: Dumping Games
requirements: A homebrewed Wii (follow the str2hax guide first if you haven't)
An SD card or USB drive with at least 4.7GB free space per disc
The game disc you want to dump
A Wii Remote and sensor bar
warnings: Dumping game discs is for backup purposes only. Do not share or distribute copyrighted game files.
A single Wii disc dump is about 4.7GB. Dual-layer discs are about 8.5GB. Make sure your storage is big enough.
CleanRip can take 30–60 minutes per disc. Don't interrupt it or the dump will be corrupted.
_note: CleanRip dumps Wii and GameCube discs to ISO format on an SD card or USB drive. The resulting files can be used with Dolphin emulator or USB Loaders.

steps.title: Before you start
steps.body:
**CleanRip** dumps your Wii and GameCube game discs to ISO files on an SD card or USB drive. You can play these backups on Dolphin emulator or via a USB Loader on the Wii itself.

You need a homebrewed Wii with the Homebrew Channel installed. If you haven't done that yet, work through the **str2hax** guide first.

You also need:
- An SD card or USB drive formatted as **FAT32** or **NTFS**
- At least **4.7GB of free space** per disc (Wii discs are 4.7GB single-layer; some are 8.5GB dual-layer; GameCube discs are 1.5GB)

Graphite handles the CleanRip download and SD card setup. You just need to run the app on the Wii.

steps.title: Put CleanRip on your SD card
steps.body:
Graphite downloads **CleanRip** and unpacks it into the `apps` folder on your SD card.

Press the button and wait for it to finish.

steps.title: Clean up and eject the card
steps.body:
macOS scatters hidden junk files (`.DS_Store`, `._` files) onto SD cards, and Wii homebrew can choke on them.

Press the button, and Graphite strips those files off the card and **safely ejects it for you**. Once it pops off, pull the card out.

steps.title: Move the SD card to your Wii
steps.body:
Take the SD card out of your computer and slot it into the **SD card slot on the front of the Wii**. If you're using a USB drive, plug it into one of the **USB ports on the back**.

Press **I did this** once the card or drive is in.

steps.title: Launch CleanRip
steps.body:
Power on the Wii and open the **Homebrew Channel**.

Look for **CleanRip** in the app grid and select it. It loads into a simple text menu.

If CleanRip doesn't appear, make sure the `apps/cleanrip` folder exists on your SD card and contains the `boot.dol` file.

steps.title: Configure CleanRip
steps.body:
CleanRip asks a few questions before it starts. Answer them with your Wii Remote:

1. **Generate a checksum?** → Select **Yes**. This verifies your dump is correct.
2. **Choose your device** → Pick your SD card or USB drive from the list.
3. **Choose the filesystem** → Select the format you used (FAT32 or NTFS).
4. **Download new.dat files?** → Select **Yes**. CleanRip fetches Redump DAT files to verify your dumps against known good hashes. This needs an internet connection.

The DAT download takes a few seconds. Once it finishes, the disc selection screen appears.

steps.title: Insert the game disc
steps.body:
Open the Wii's disc drive and insert the game disc you want to dump. Close the lid.

CleanRip detects the disc and shows you its type and size. Press **I did this** once the disc is in.

steps.title: Start the dump
steps.body:
CleanRip asks you to select the disc type. Wii discs are usually **Wii** (not GameCube). If the disc is a dual-layer game (like *Super Smash Bros. Brawl*), select **Dual Layer**.

Choose your disc type and confirm. The dump begins — it shows progress bars for reading and checksumming.

!!Do NOT turn off the Wii or remove the SD card while dumping!!.

This takes **30–60 minutes** depending on the disc. Go do something else. If CleanRip finds errors during the read, it retries automatically.

troubleshoot.label: The dump failed or has errors

steps.title: You're done
steps.body:
CleanRip has saved your game dump to the root of your SD card or USB drive. The file is named after the game's **disc ID** (e.g. `RSBE01.iso` for *Super Smash Bros. Brawl*).

If your drive is formatted as **FAT32**, large files are split into **PART** files (`.part1`, `.part2`, etc.) because FAT32 can't hold files over 4GB. To rejoin them on a computer:

```
copy /b game.iso.part1 + game.iso.part2 + game.iso.part3 game.iso
```

On macOS:
```
cat game.iso.part* > game.iso
```

You can now use the ISO with **Dolphin emulator** or copy it to a USB loader on the Wii for playing on real hardware.
